Day 4 — PWA First Look: Architecture (Part 2, Service Workers)
Part 2 of PWA architecture. This one's about the core of PWA — Service Workers.

Service Workers are the heart of PWA, mainly because they unlock offline caching. They do other powerful things too, but for a web dev, offline caching alone is huge. Earlier I mentioned that web pages typically die without a network connection and you get the offline dinosaur:
What do we do when the page is offline or has flaky connectivity?
Click the dinosaur and play Super Chrome Dino to kill time?
Service Workers exist so you don't have to play
. As I mentioned, the power is offline-caching images, JS, etc. Here's an old Vue project of mine, RagnarokShopV3, in PWA mode — you can see a bunch of stuff cached in the browser:
Also, the Service Worker is a JavaScript file.

You read that right — Service Workers have their own lifecycle. They're a kind of JavaScript, but unlike regular JavaScript they can't directly manipulate the DOM. A Service Worker is more like a background script (you
manipulate the DOM if you really want, but I won't cover that here). It's a fully independent JavaScript file.
The Google docs have important notes about Service Workers, which I'll just quote:
and can't access the DOM directly. The Service Worker communicates with the pages it controls via the
interface, and the page can do DOM work as needed.
The Service Worker is a programmable network proxy that lets you control how network requests from a page are handled.
It's terminated when not in use and restarted when needed, so you can't rely on global state inside the
handlers. To persist data across restarts, Service Workers can use the
Service Workers use Promises extensively, so if you're not comfortable with Promises, stop and read the

Honestly, the Service Worker lifecycle clicks faster when you actually build with it, so I'm not going to dump the whole API surface here. But before using Service Workers, two things to watch out for:

Service Worker support is rolling out across browsers. Per Google's docs, Firefox and Opera already support it. Microsoft Edge has publicly stated it'll support it. Safari hasn't publicly committed but is hinting at it. As for IE — Microsoft has abandoned it, so don't worry about IE (
Microsoft: Stop using IE as a browser
Service Workers only run over HTTPS.
works too (otherwise how would you develop?). If your server's on HTTP, you'll need to migrate to HTTPS.
